answer:
The vibrational partition function for a diatomic molecule is given by:

\[
Z_v = \frac{e^{-\theta_v / (2T)}}{1 - e^{-\theta_v / T}}
\]

where \(\theta_v = \frac{hc\omega}{k_B} = \frac{hc \cdot 323.2 \, \text{cm}^{-1}}{k_B}\) is the vibrational temperature, and \(T\) is the temperature in Kelvin.

The approximate formula (high-temperature limit) is \(Z_v \approx T/\theta_v\), valid when \(T \gg \theta_v\). For \(\mathrm{Br}_2\), \(\theta_v \approx 465 \, \text{K}\), so the exact and approximate values converge when \(T \gg 465 \, \text{K}\). Testing the options, **5000 K** (Option I) is the lowest temperature where the exact \(Z_v\) is within 5% of the approximate value
